summary: Spolsky applies the microeconomic concept of complements to explain corporate technology strategy, particularly around open source software. He argues that smart companies deliberately try to commoditize their products' complements to increase demand and pricing power for their own products. He demonstrates this through examples including IBM commoditizing PC add-ins, Microsoft commoditizing PC hardware, and major corporations funding open source development. He debunks the notion that companies support open source out of idealism, showing it's a rational economic strategy. He also argues that open source software is not exempt from economic laws, and that 'free' code still has real costs in developer time and opportunity cost. The essay concludes by noting that software can easily commoditize hardware, but the reverse is extremely difficult due to switching costs.
key_points: Demand for a product increases when the price of its complements decreases, so smart companies try to commoditize their complements to boost their own profits.

Most corporations investing in open source are doing so as a deliberate business strategy to commoditize software that complements their core business, not out of ideological commitment to free software.
Open source software is not exempt from economics: debugged code has real costs in developer time and opportunity cost, even when no money changes hands.
Software can easily commoditize hardware through abstraction layers, but hardware cannot easily commoditize software because of switching costs and non-interchangeability.
Sun Microsystems is pursuing a contradictory strategy by simultaneously trying to commoditize both software and hardware, leaving itself with no proprietary advantage.
money_quotes: Smart companies try to commoditize their products' complements.
Demand for a product increases when the prices of its complements decrease.

Most of the companies spending big money to develop open source software are doing it because it's a good business strategy for them, not because they suddenly stopped believing in capitalism and fell in love with freedom-as-in-speech.
Debugged code is NOT free, whether proprietary or open source. Even if you don't pay cash dollars for it, it has opportunity cost, and it has time cost.
I'm not very impressed by people who try to prove wild economic things about free-as-in-beer software, because they're just getting divide-by-zero errors as far as I'm concerned.

Without proprietary advantages in hardware or software, you're going to have to take the commodity price, which barely covers the cost of cheap factories in Guadalajara, not your cushy offices in Silicon Valley.
Even when the price is zero, the cost of switching from Microsoft Office is non-zero. Until the switching cost becomes zero, desktop office software is not truly a commodity.
And that's why Bill Gates can buy Sweden and you can't.
key_insight: Companies invest in open source not out of altruism but as a deliberate strategy to commoditize their complements, thereby increasing demand and pricing power for their core products.
